steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The passage ends with a claim that needs a logical completion: the finding that Kwakwaka'wakw songs accurately describe productive clam-garden techniques (confirmed by archaeological and paleoecological records) 'suggests that researchers should consider ______.' The blank must follow logically from the evidence that Indigenous songs can contain accurate ecological knowledge.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — the strong implication that the beneficial effects are primarily attributable to ecological factors unrelated to the activities of the Kwakwaka'wakw people: The passage explicitly ties the increases in clam size to the gardens created by the Kwakwaka'wakw. This directly contradicts the evidence. ✗
  • Choice B — the possibility that other songs of other Indigenous peoples transmit ecological information that has useful practical applications: If one group's songs contained accurate, practically useful ecological knowledge, it follows logically that songs from other Indigenous peoples might too. This is the reasonable generalization the finding supports. ✓
  • Choice C — the reliability of archaeological and paleoecological evidence that often underpins conclusions drawn from Indigenous songs: The passage uses archaeological and paleoecological evidence to confirm the songs, not the other way around. The finding is about the songs' value, not about validating the evidence. ✗
  • Choice D — whether the Kwakwaka'wakw people originated the method of cultivation detailed in the songs or adapted methods observed among other groups of Indigenous peoples: The passage says nothing about the origins of the technique; this question is outside the scope of the finding. ✗
That confirms B is correct.
